design: EVIDENCE-SPLIT HYBRID: n=22 taught forms + 8 compositions, tiered into 6 language primitives (branded ids, margin buffers, layout views, SIMD core, machine kit, concurrency core), ~15 sealed builtin-TCB kernels (vec/map/omap/pool/channels/mutex/rings/allocator/IO), and checked-library kernels (sort/search/hash/format/cursor) riding requires-prologue elision; ~47 new spec rules; claims 44 PAR / 1 WIN / 6 PARTIAL on the 51-scenario demand map.
lens: PERFORMANCE HOLES — common workloads where no blessed spelling plausibly reaches the named SOTA par: extra indirections, missed SIMD, forced lookups/copies/checks, missed inlining, plus performance predictability and the optimizer-facts story.

findings.title: The sealed-kernel/optimizer boundary is unspecified, and every Tier-B par number silently assumes intrinsic-grade inlining
findings.detail: The design's headline numbers — vec push ~1 ns amortized, deque push/pop ~2-3 ns, pool get ~1-2 ns, omap ordered scan within 2x of vec iteration, map hit ~2 cache lines — are only reachable if the sealed op bodies (4-5 instructions for push: cap compare, store, len increment) inline into the caller's loop and participate in its register allocation, LICM, and vectorization. Rust Vec gets this because the optimizer sees the source. But the sealed-form mechanism is defined as 'opaque builtin type families with fixed op signatures... no reflection into internals; op calls typecheck as ordinary function calls.' If ops compile as ABI calls, push becomes ~5-10 ns (call/ret, clobbered registers, no loop-carried len in a register, no autovectorized iteration through as_view-free paths) — a 5-10x regression on the highest-weight scenario in the map (F1 vector), and omap/map cursor iteration via per-element next()->option calls cannot hit the 'near-streaming leaf walk' claim at all. If instead the ops DO inline as compiler-known intrinsics, then the optimizer is reasoning about TCB internals interleaved with checked code, and the margin/uninit-extent invariants ('writes past len unobservable', dead-store elimination over spare capacity) must be re-proved against every optimization pass — a design obligation nowhere in the 47 rules or the TCB ledger. Evidence to settle: state the lowering model, then asm-diff a 1M-u64 append-then-sum loop against Rust Vec; the design currently cannot say which of the two worlds it is in.

findings.title: No-reborrow turns multi-op mutation through a container into a fresh lookup per operation: map<K, vec<V>> accumulation pays a hash+probe per pushed element
findings.detail: A unique borrow is consumed by a call and cannot be reborrowed. entry()/find_uniq() return &uniq V; the first op call on that V (e.g., inner.push(x), or any two sequential method calls) consumes it, and there is no way to get it back except a new map probe. The single most common associative idiom after plain get — grouping/accumulation into map<K, vec<V>> (hash-join build sides, word/event counting into lists, adjacency-list construction, DB group-by, compiler use-lists) — therefore costs one SwissTable probe (~25-40 ns DRAM-resident) per appended element instead of per key, versus hashbrown's ~1 ns push after one entry() — a 20-40x hole on the probe-bound portion. The design's own entry() source_shape only survives because its arm does exactly one field assignment; any value work requiring two op calls (push then check len; update two nested containers; call a helper on &uniq V) re-probes. The blessed workarounds are all worse or different: command-buffer (materialize edits, second pass, extra allocation), sort-then-group (fine for batch, not for streaming/incremental), or value-as-handle into a pool (adds a generation-checked indirection per touch). Evidence: write the wordcount-into-lists benchmark in blessed xlang vs hashbrown entry(); count probes per element. Fix would be a borrow-session rule (an op that returns the same &uniq it took), which is a real language delta the design does not list.

findings.title: Env structs cannot hold borrows, which contradicts the design's own load-bearing shapes: find_by comparators over arenas, borrowed-LUT parallel bodies, and context-dependent hashers are unwritable as specified
findings.detail: The kernel digest is explicit: borrows CANNOT be stored in structs/data, and env structs are ordinary structs. Yet the design's mechanisms require exactly that: (a) interning's map.find_by(env, keyview) needs a comparator/hasher env holding &arena to resolve (offset,len) spans — without it, the map's keys must own their bytes, reinstating the malloc-per-unique-string and pointer-chasing probes the demand map names as the 2x fail; (b) the parallel_for source_shape literally writes Body { lut: &lut } — a borrow stored in a struct; every kernel parameterized by a read-only table (classifier LUTs, sort-by-key over a separate key array/argsort, comparator with tie-break state) has the same need; (c) streaming hashers with borrowed seeds/state. Either the design adds region-annotated borrow-carrying structs — a substantial checker delta (region parameters on struct types, variance, escape at store sites) conspicuously absent from the 47-rule budget and adjacent to the 'no Rust-scale inference' feasibility ruling — or the interning PAR row, the find_by op, and the parallel_for-with-shared-input WIN/PAR rows all degrade to copy-the-context-into-the-env (fine for scalars, a full data copy for LUTs/key arrays: argsort over 10M keys would copy 80MB into each env or pack (key,idx) pairs at 2x memory traffic). Evidence: attempt to typecheck Body { lut: &lut } under the stated rules; there is no third outcome.

findings.title: The design's only WIN row is internally incoherent: float reduction fails the exact associativity refuter, and overflow-trapping int reduction breaks deterministic traps under reassociation
findings.detail: The WIN claims parallel_reduce legally reassociates/vectorizes 'including float and overflow-checked-int reductions' because the monoid's assoc law is machine-checked. But the language's contract mechanism REFUTES false laws at compile time, and f64 addition is not associative — an exact refuter (random/exhaustive small-float counterexample search finds (1e308 + -1e308) + 1 != 1e308 + (-1e308 + 1) instantly) must reject SumMonoid<f64>, so the float win cannot exist without weakening the refuter into an approximate check, i.e., opening a fact channel that licenses result-changing transforms — exactly what the constitution forbids. For overflow-checked ints, reassociation changes WHICH inputs trap (a+b overflows where a+(b+c) does not) and parallel split changes trap timing/identity, violating 'failures trap deterministically' unless the semantics of trapping reduction is redefined to 'traps iff some association traps' — a spec change not in the rule budget. What remains of the WIN is wrapping/modular integer reduction, which LLVM already reassociates and vectorizes legally for C/C++/Rust today — no advantage. The measured 3.3x reassociation result does not rescue this; the question is which element types survive the design's own law checker. Evidence: state the refuter's decision procedure and run it on SumMonoid<f64>; the coverage table then reads 45 PAR / 0 WIN, which materially weakens the P0 'Rust test' story.

findings.title: The sealed map forbids software-pipelined (group) prefetching, conceding the documented 2-4x on DRAM-resident probe loops — hash joins, hash aggregation, dedup at scale
findings.detail: F9-5's own PAR row cites group prefetching: compute N probe addresses, prefetcht0 all, then process N, keeping 8-16 misses in flight for 2-4x probe throughput on >100M-entry tables (Chen et al., DuckDB/Hyper lineage). That requires translating a key's hash to a slot/control-byte ADDRESS before the probe — precisely the internal fact the sealed SwissTable hides, and no op in the map's stated contract (insert/find/find_uniq/remove/entry/find_by) exposes a prefetch_key(h) or two-phase hash-then-probe interface. In Rust this is reachable via hashbrown's raw-entry/RawTable APIs; in blessed xlang the analytics-shaped workload (hash join build+probe is THE database kernel; the demand map weights the general hash map HIGH) is structurally stuck at one outstanding miss, i.e., the map's own fail-shape arithmetic: ~90 ns serial dependent miss per probe vs ~25-40 ns pipelined. The prefetch primitive being vacuous-safe is irrelevant if there is no address to feed it. Evidence: benchmark a 200M-entry u64 join probe in blessed xlang vs a hand-pipelined hashbrown loop; a fix is one added sealed op (prefetch(k) that hashes and touches the control line), which the design should preregister rather than discover post-ship.

findings.title: Requires-prologue elision cannot discharge data-dependent/quantified index facts, leaving per-element checks (and vectorization blocks) in radix scatter, CSR traversal, histogram, and table-driven decode loops
findings.detail: The elision evidence (27/27 on base64, memchr-style hoisting) covers cursor-monotone bounds tied to argument lengths. The scenarios claimed PAR that do NOT have that shape: (a) radix sort scatter — out[offsets[digit]++] = x, where in-boundedness rests on a prefix-sum correctness invariant; the checker explicitly does no invariant synthesis, so each scatter store keeps a checked index; at 4-8 streaming passes of ~3-5 cycles/element, a compare+branch plus the ordering constraint of a potentially-trapping store per element is a 15-30% tax per pass on the row that claims 'internal radix specialization' at voracious-class 2.5x; (b) CSR BFS/DFS — edge targets loaded from data index the visited/nodes arrays; 'all stored edge ids < n' is a quantified container invariant no syntax-directed rule carries (branded ids fix this only for arena-allocated graphs, not the frozen vec<u32> CSR the coverage row prescribes); (c) FSE/Huffman and interpreter dispatch tables — symbol-driven table indices. Beyond the branch cost, a trapping load/store inside the loop pins trap order and defeats the autovectorization/reordering the PAR rows assume. Evidence: the elision report on a counting-sort scatter loop and a CSR BFS inner loop — if checks survive, the sort and graph PAR rows need re-measurement with checks on, and the design needs either a proved-once 'all elements < n' vec refinement type (a new delta) or must downgrade those rows.

findings.title: Margin is an allocation-provenance property, but the checked F5 kernels demand it on inputs that commonly cannot supply it: filemap views, subslices, and borrowed wire buffers
findings.detail: EXP-2's memchr signature is bytes<'r, margin 32>, and the SIMD core legalizes masked/wide tail loads only under margin or discharged bounds. But the flagship consumers hand you unmargined bytes: (a) filemap views — an mmap'd file's last page ends at a page boundary; bytes past EOF fault, so a filemap view can never carry margin 32, yet ripgrep-over-mmap and SQLite-page scanning are the named workloads; (b) subslices — a validated frame body c.take(len) or v.as_view()[a..b] has margin only if b+32 <= allocation end, a dynamic fact the frontend-simple checker cannot carry, so margin dies at the first slice even over margined vecs; (c) network buffers filled to exact length. Consequence: every F5 kernel forks into a margined fast version and an unmargined version with masked-load-under-proved-bounds or scalar tails — either two spellings per kernel (R3 violation, D2 spec growth, and the weak-writer picks wrong) or a copy-into-margined-buffer at the boundary (memcpy pass the SOTA doesn't pay: for a 100MB mmap'd haystack that is the whole win gone). The SOTA trick these kernels replace — 'overread within the same page is harmless' — is exactly what margin was supposed to encode, and it doesn't compose through the two most common byte-view sources. Evidence: typecheck memchr(filemap.view(), n) as designed; then measure the unmargined-tail variant on 16-64B inputs (HTTP field scans) vs memchr crate.

findings.title: Predictability cliffs the catalog itself creates: the two-spelling get-or-insert and the silent INLINE=0 default
findings.detail: R3 promises one spelling per meaning, but the map ships find + insert as separate ops alongside entry(); a modest writer's natural composition — match m.find(k) { none => m.insert(k,v), ... } — is legal, looks idiomatic, and costs 2x on the counter/cache workloads the entry token exists for (two hashes + two probes, ~50-80 ns vs ~25-40 ns DRAM-resident). Nothing rejects or lints it. Similarly, the 3-5x SmallVector win (HIGH weight, LLVM-shaped workloads: millions of 2-8 element operand lists) hides behind an INLINE parameter defaulting to 0 with no signal in the 3-question decision tree ('sequence -> vec' never asks about expected n or churn), so the weak writer ships the malloc-per-list shape and never learns why the compiler workload is 3x off. Both are exactly the 'wrong-but-legal choice falls off a cliff' failure the W1 story claims the closed catalog prevents. Evidence: give a mid-tier model the spec and the wordcount + operand-list tasks; count how often it produces the slow legal spelling. Cheap fixes exist (make find+insert-after-none a checker lint; make the decision tree ask expected-n), but they must be designed in, not assumed.

findings.title: Without any shared-ownership mechanism, snapshot-cell publish is an O(n) rebuild, so 'arc-swap-class' PAR overstates the read-copy fallback for tables with nontrivial update rates
findings.detail: shared<T>.publish(v) MOVES a whole new value in; xlang has no Arc/Rc and borrows cannot be stored, so building the next version of a 1M-entry routing/session table means a full clone+modify (~tens of ms of memcpy and rehash) per update — there is no structural sharing of unchanged substructure, which is how arc-swap/evmap users actually amortize (Arc'd shards or persistent maps). At even 10 updates/s on a 1M-entry table the publisher burns a core and evicts caches; the blessed fallback is the sharded mutex map with its acknowledged ~1.5-2x read tax. The design already marks papaya-class as PARTIAL, but the 'read-copy tables at arc-swap-class' line in two coverage rows quietly assumes update rates near zero; stated honestly, the read-mostly hole is wider than the one admitted row. Evidence: benchmark a 1M-entry table at 1, 10, 100 updates/s: publish-side cost and reader-side p99 vs arc-swap-over-Arc-shards; the fix direction (persistent/COW sealed map, or Arc'd sealed shards) is a new kernel the TCB ledger should anticipate.

overall: The stance is rescuable — the three-tier criterion is sound, the sealed-kernel bet is the right answer for SwissTable/pool/Chase-Lev, and most Tier-B rows genuinely inherit their SOTA mechanism — but the design has a systematic blind spot: it validates forms as isolated op signatures while par lives in the seams between checked code and sealed internals, and at least seven common workloads fall into those seams (uninlined op calls, per-op re-probes under no-reborrow, no prefetchable addresses, undischargeable data-dependent bounds, margin that dies at subslice/mmap boundaries, borrow-less env structs contradicting the design's own source shapes, and a WIN row its own law-refuter should reject). The single most important change: specify the sealed-form/optimizer interaction contract — intrinsic inlining semantics, multi-op borrow sessions on container ops, and hot-path auxiliary ops (prefetch_key, two-phase probes) — as first-class design surface with asm-diff gates, because findings 1, 2, and 5 are all the same root defect: the interface was designed for safety typing, and performance parity is being asserted across a boundary whose codegen semantics were never written down. Secondarily, resolve the env-struct borrow contradiction explicitly (it is a rule-budget item, not a footnote) and re-run the coverage table with the WIN row honestly downgraded; a 45-PAR/0-WIN hybrid that names these seams and preregisters their experiments is still the strongest stance on the table.

design: design:hybrid (EVIDENCE-SPLIT HYBRID: 6 language deltas / ~15 sealed builtin-TCB kernels / checked-library tier, n=22 forms + 8 compositions, ~47 new spec rules)
lens: SAFETY & TCB — forgeable authority at API boundaries, aliasing/invalidation across mutation, callback reentrancy and trap paths, whether "checked" claims type under the frozen ownership rules (no reborrow, no borrows in data, affine moves), and honesty/verifiability of the TCB ledger

findings.title: Entry vacancy token survives intervening map mutation: stale-slot write inside the sealed SwissTable kernel
findings.detail: The design's own source shape proves the hole: `match m.entry(k) { vacant(t) => { m.insert_at(t, ...) } }` requires `m` to be re-borrowable after `entry()` returns — under the no-reborrow rule the unique borrow taken by `entry()` is consumed by the call, so for `m.insert_at(t, v)` to typecheck, the map borrow must have ENDED when `entry()` returned. But then nothing stops `m.insert(k2, v2)` (triggering growth/rehash, slot relocation, control-byte rewrite) between `entry()` and `insert_at(t, ...)`. The token `t` encodes a probe result (group index / slot offset) into the pre-rehash table; `insert_at` is sealed and, per the one-hash-one-probe perf claim, writes to the cached slot without re-probing — a write into a reallocated or repurposed slot: memory corruption manufactured entirely from safe source, i.e., a forged authority ('this slot is vacant in THIS table version') crossing the TCB boundary. The claimed fix ('tok is region-tied') cannot work with lexical regions: the region does not name the map binding, and making the token pin the map is exactly reborrowing, which the language bans; making insert_at re-validate (re-hash+re-probe) forfeits the one-probe claim EXP-1 preregisters. The `occupied(&uniq V)` arm has the sibling problem: if the map borrow ended at return (needed for the vacant arm to type), the `&uniq V` aliases a container the writer can now mutate/remove from. Falsify: attempt to write typing rules for the design's own printed example that both accept it and reject the insert-between variant — no frontend-simple (flow-free) rule can distinguish them.

findings.title: Load-bearing APIs store borrows in data, which the frozen language bans: env-struct captures and scoped-spawn borrowed environments do not typecheck
findings.detail: The digest's settled rules: borrows CANNOT be stored in structs/data; env structs are ordinary structs. Yet at least four claimed-checked forms depend on borrow-carrying env structs: (1) parallel_for's own source shape `Body { lut: &lut }`; (2) the interner's `find_by` comparator env, which must reference the arena byte store to resolve (offset,len) spans; (3) scoped spawn 'borrows 'p-region data' — the env moved to the worker thread must carry those borrows; (4) condvar `wait(guard, pred_env)` predicates over shared state. Either these are ill-typed (then the WIN row for data parallelism, the interning PAR row, the sharded-map PAR row, and crossbeam-scope-class spawn all silently lose their stated shapes), or the design smuggles in an unlisted delta — region-parameterized borrow-carrying structs — which is precisely the lifetime-in-struct machinery the 'checker stays frontend-simple, no Rust-scale inference' feasibility ruling rejected, and which reopens the dangling-borrow-in-data hole the ban exists to close (an env struct with a borrow moved into a pool/vec outlives its region). The ~8-rule concurrency-core estimate contains no such mechanism. Falsify: elaborate `parallel_for(data.as_view_uniq(), 4096, Body { lut: &lut })` under the 90-rule kernel plus the listed deltas; it must be rejected.

findings.title: id<'r,T> brands name the region, not the arena: two arenas in one region alias brands, giving check-free out-of-bounds deref
findings.detail: The delta says brand matching is 'syntactic equality of the region variable' (~3 rules). Then `region 'a { let ar1 = arena.new(); let ar2 = arena.new(); }` yields two values of the SAME type arena<'a,Expr>; an id minted by 1000 allocations into ar1 typechecks against `ar2[id]`, which is DEFINED check-free — base + id*stride into an arena holding 1 element: raw OOB read/write, T1 broken by safe source. Same attack via function boundaries: `fn f<'a>(x: &arena<'a,E>, y: &arena<'a,E>, i: id<'a,E>)` — any caller with two same-region arenas confuses them. Soundness requires GENERATIVE per-arena-value brands (each arena.new() mints a fresh existential region), which is a materially bigger mechanism than region-variable syntactic equality: it needs fresh-brand introduction, brand flow through function signatures, and interaction rules with the region staircase — decidable, but not the 3 rules costed, and the honest version also constrains the useful pattern of passing several arenas of one region to a builder. Falsify: write the two-arena program against the proposed typing rules; if it typechecks, the check-free deref rule is unsound as specified.

findings.title: Margin-buffer semantics is self-contradictory and is a live fact channel: codec correctness needs margin bytes observable, while tail SIMD loads can launder stale heap bytes into discharged bounds facts
findings.detail: Two concrete failures. (a) LZ4 wild copy: a 32B store past len writes real match bytes into margin; the decoder then advances len OVER those bytes and later overlapping `copy_within` reads them back. If 'writes into margin are semantically discarded' the optimizer may legally dead-store-eliminate the margin lanes, corrupting output once len advances — so the semantics as written breaks EXP-3's own mechanism; if instead margin writes are real and become observable on len advance, the 'never observable as initialized data' claim is false and needs a genuine info-flow argument (margin slop comes from allocator/pool recycling WITHOUT re-zeroing, so margin bytes are another object's freed contents). (b) Fact laundering in the memchr tail: load 32B at the tail (legal under margin 32), compare, movemask — if masking of margin lanes is the kernel author's obligation rather than enforced by typing, a stale margin byte equal to the needle yields an index >= len; downstream code that treats 'index returned by find < len' as a requires-fact gets its bounds checks discharged against a false fact — proof-elision built on garbage, exactly the fact-channel class the standing rules demand hostile review for. Evidence to settle: a written small-step semantics for margin views that simultaneously (i) permits DSE-safe wild copies, (ii) defines len-advance over previously-margin bytes, (iii) forces margin-lane masking in every mask-to-scalar bridge — plus an adversarial kernel showing what the checker does when the mask is omitted.

findings.title: The share/send judgment as specified excludes the design's own concurrency catalog; the correct version is conditional Send/Sync-style reasoning, not 'a Copy bit'
findings.detail: Stated rule: shareable-by-& iff the type 'transitively contains no sealed interior-mutable cell'. But mutex<T>, counter, flag, shared<T>, and the mpmc endpoints ARE sealed interior-mutable cells — under the stated judgment none of them is shareable, so no thread can ever touch them through &, and the whole F7 story collapses. The real judgment must be conditional: mutex<T> shareable iff T sendable; shared<T> readable iff T shareable; spsc tx<T> sendable iff T sendable; a read-guard &T must be non-sendable; handle<T> is Copy and freely sendable but must not license cross-thread pool access. These conditional per-form rules are exactly the auto-trait surface where Rust's ecosystem produced its worst soundness CVEs (MutexGuard: Sync, scoped-thread leaks), and 'computed exactly like a Copy bit' materially understates them. Since the DRF-by-construction theorem — the design's admitted single point of catastrophic failure — quantifies over this judgment, an underspecified judgment means the theorem currently has no precise statement to review. Falsify: write the per-sealed-form share/send table and check it makes (i) mutex<spsc_rx<T>> sound, (ii) copy struct containing a counter rejected, (iii) snapshot guards region-confined; the stated one-bit rule fails (i) trivially.

findings.title: Trap semantics through sealed kernels is unresolved: user callbacks (hasher, comparator, monoid) trapping mid-operation either demand unwind-safety verification of every kernel or contradict the parallel runtime's contract
findings.detail: The digest gives two incompatible readings: mutex says 'a trap while holding is a deterministic process-boundary trap' (trap = process death, no poisoning needed), while the parallel runtime says join 'propagates its trap to the boundary' (trap = catchable unwind crossing the TCB). If traps unwind: a checked hasher trapping inside sealed rehash (table half-migrated, some slots moved-out), a comparator trapping inside sort (elements in scratch), or a monoid trapping mid-parallel_reduce (partial results owned by dead workers) each leaves a sealed kernel mid-mutation — every one of the ~15 kernels then carries an exception-safety obligation (restore coherent state, run drops of half-moved T exactly once) that the TCB verification story (model checking, differential fuzzing) never mentions and that fuzzing against panic-free oracles will not exercise. If traps abort the process: the parallel runtime's stated join contract is false, and condvar predicates/completion-ring cancellation lose their error paths. Falsify: one paragraph of spec stating what a trap in a callback invoked by a sealed op does to (a) the container, (b) sibling workers, (c) values mid-move — and a trap-injection suite added to the kernel verification plan. Until it exists, 'never UB, deterministic traps' is asserted for exactly the paths nobody plans to test.

findings.title: Completion ring teardown with in-flight kernel-owned buffers has no typed or stated story: io_uring DMA into freed memory on drop
findings.detail: submit() moves the vec into the op — sound for the happy path. But the loan's INVERSE has no contract: if the ring is dropped (or its region ends, or the process unwinds per finding above) while ops are in flight, io_uring semantics require cancel + await-completion before buffer memory may be reused; freeing the vec's storage immediately means the kernel completes a DMA write into memory the allocator has recycled — heap corruption originating in the TCB with no writer error at all. Affine tokens don't help: dropping the completion token is legal affine behavior, and the buffer is then owned by nobody visible. The TCB must implement synchronous drain-on-drop (unbounded blocking in a destructor — new semantics the spec must state) or orphan-buffer parking (buffers leak until completion — must be stated), and neither appears in the I/O kernel's ~4k LOC contract or its verification plan; differential fuzzing 'against liburing' will not test teardown races because liburing delegates exactly this discipline to the caller. Falsify: state the ring-drop/token-drop/region-exit contract and add a teardown-under-load stress test with MADV_FREE-style allocator recycling; absent that, the 'buffer loans are static ownership facts' claim is only half a proof.

findings.title: The TCB ledger is underestimated ~3-5x and omits whole trusted subsystems, which undermines the 'hostile review + exhaustive model checking' verification promise
findings.detail: Line items vs reality: mimalloc 2.x core is ~7-10k LOC of subtle C (claimed 4-5k); the 'completion ring lowering to io_uring OR epoll+nonblocking emulation' is a portable async I/O driver — the corresponding layers of libuv/tokio-driver are tens of kLOC, budgeted here inside a 4k line item shared with buffered files, filemap, and the OS facade; the scoped-thread + work-stealing runtime (worker lifecycle, futex sleep/wake, stealing, trap propagation) is rayon-core-class, budgeted inside 3k shared with five other concurrency kernels. Unlisted TCB entirely: the SIGBUS-to-deterministic-trap signal handler (async faults in optimized code); the strict-tier select 'verified never-branch' verifier (a per-ISA post-codegen asm checker — nothing else in the toolchain does this today); per-ISA masked-load/margin lowering correctness; the trap/unwind runtime if traps propagate. The verification claims also overreach: loom/GenMC-style exhaustive checking does not model futex parking, signals, or io_uring kernel interaction, so the kernels' hardest paths (parking races, teardown, trap unwind) sit outside the advertised method. This matters under this lens because the design's safety argument IS the ledger: 'small, enumerated, each item verifiable'. Falsify: produce the per-kernel LOC ledger against real reference implementations and a per-kernel map of which verification method covers which contract clause; the current totals will not survive it.

findings.title: filemap's 'SIGBUS converted to a defined deterministic trap' is not deliverable as stated and quietly constrains the optimizer
findings.detail: External truncation is asynchronous third-party action, so WHEN the fault fires is inherently nondeterministic; worse, layout views guarantee 'single load, zero residual checks', which invites the optimizer to hoist, widen, or vectorize mapped-memory loads — after which a SIGBUS arrives at an instruction with no precise source-level attribution, and 'deterministic trap' degenerates to 'we abort somewhere near the access'. Making it precise requires either marking every mapped-region load non-hoistable/non-mergeable (taxing exactly the LMDB-class warm-read numbers the row cites) or accepting an imprecise-fault semantics the spec must own. Small surface, but it is the one place an external process can inject a fault into 'checked' code, so the wording should be honest: bounded imprecise trap, or mandatory advisory-lock discipline with SIGBUS as declared TCB-mediated abort. Falsify: write the trap-attribution rule for a vectorized loop over a filemap view and check it against the codegen-parity gate.

overall: The stance is rescuable — the three-tier criterion, the sealed-kernel discipline for implement-rarely structures, and the linearity-based SPSC/loan story are genuinely the right architecture — but the design as written has not been elaborated against xlang's own frozen ownership rules, and three of its most load-bearing 'checked' surfaces (the one-probe entry token, borrow-carrying env structs for parallel_for/interning/scoped-spawn, and region-branded arena ids) either fail to typecheck or typecheck unsoundly under no-reborrow / no-borrows-in-data / region-equals-brand semantics. The single change that matters most: before any experiment, mechanically type-elaborate every printed source_shape under the 90 rules plus the costed deltas, and where elaboration fails, either add the honest missing delta (generative per-value brands; a region-confined borrow-carrying token/env form — accepting the checker growth that implies) or demote the API to a sealed two-probe/moved-capture shape and re-price the coverage rows. Secondarily, the margin semantics needs a written small-step definition that survives DSE and forced mask discharge before it can be called a fact channel at all, and the TCB ledger needs re-costing against real reference implementations with a per-clause verification map — the current 15k/exhaustive-model-checking framing is the kind of optimistic accounting the project's own standing rules exist to catch.

design: design:hybrid — EVIDENCE-SPLIT HYBRID (22 taught forms + 8 composition patterns; Tier A language primitives / Tier B sealed builtin-TCB / Tier C checked library; claimed +47 kernel rules, 90 -> ~137)
lens: SPEC SIZE, TEACHABILITY, EVOLUTION — honest rule recount, mid-tier-writer usability from spec alone, first out-of-catalog program, fit with ANF/match-only/no-reborrow/no-borrows-in-data kernel, elegance vs uniformed special cases

findings.title: The form APIs are untypeable in the existing kernel: borrows inside enums, options, guards, and cursors are pervasive, and the kernel bans borrows in data
findings.detail: The current kernel rules: borrows CANNOT be stored in structs/data, no reborrowing, a unique borrow is consumed by a call. Yet the design's interface surface returns borrow-carrying data everywhere: map.find -> option<&V>, find_uniq -> option<&uniq V>, pool.get(h) -> option<&T>, entry(k) -> enum{occupied(&uniq V), vacant(tok)}, mutex.lock() -> guard granting &uniq T (a borrow held in a struct across statements), omap.range -> a cursor whose next() yields option<(&K,&V)> while the cursor itself holds a borrow of the tree across loop iterations, ring.next_completion() -> a tuple carrying a token, a result, AND a moved buffer. Every one of these is the exact shape the frontend-simplicity ruling excluded. There are only two fixes: (a) a new 'transient borrow-carrying value' category — region-tied, non-storable, must-be-matched-immediately values — which is a new rule family (plausibly 8-15 rules: introduction per op class, elimination via match, interaction with move/consume, invalidation on container mutation, cursor-liveness vs push/remove) that appears NOWHERE in the 47-rule count and directly contradicts the checker_story claim that 'the checker does nothing new at all' for these ops; or (b) each borrow-returning op becomes its own primitive match-headed syntactic form, which multiplies spelling count per form and shreds the elegance claim. Evidence that settles it: attempt to write typing derivations for map.find, entry/insert_at, pool.get_uniq, and a full omap.range loop using only v0.6's 90 rules plus the listed 47 deltas; I predict at least four underivable judgments. Until this protocol is designed, every coverage row that says PAR is a claim about pseudocode in a language that does not exist.

findings.title: Spec-mass undercount of roughly 3-5x: sealed op signatures ARE spec under W1/D2, and the catalog appendix is 12-20k tokens, not 4-6k
findings.detail: The accounting trick is 'the general sealed mechanism is 3 rules; op signatures live in the taught catalog appendix, not the kernel.' But W1 requires a mid-tier writer to produce correct code from the in-context spec — so every op signature, failure row, effect row, region-tie, and affinity annotation must be in context; whether it is labeled kernel or appendix is irrelevant to D2. Honest op enumeration: vec ~12 ops (new/push/pop/index r+w/reserve/try_reserve/truncate/as_view/as_view_uniq/fill_via/bulk-copy-NT/iteration), deque +5, map ~11 (incl. entry, insert_at, find_by with env comparator, hasher contract binding), omap ~9 + cursor semantics, pool ~7, mutex/once/condvar ~7, spsc/mpmc ~9 (incl. batched reserve/commit — itself a two-phase affine protocol), shared/counter/flag ~6, scope/spawn/parallel_for/parallel_reduce/wsdeque ~9, file I/O ~12, completion ring ~10 (op kinds x multishot x cancel x timers), filemap ~4, OS facade ~8, plus the SIMD named-intrinsic table with a per-ISA availability matrix (a 2D table that is pure spec mass). That is ~120-130 ops; at spec precision (signature + preconditions + failure semantics + effect row + region/affinity discipline) each costs 60-120 tokens, giving 10-16k tokens for the appendix alone, before the 8 composition patterns, the decision tree, and worked ANF examples. In rule-equivalents the true delta is ~150-250, i.e. the kernel does not go 90 -> 137, it goes 90 -> 250-350 effective. Evidence: fully transcribe just THREE forms (map, ring, parallel runtime) at the precision the existing 90 rules are written at, count tokens, extrapolate; compare against the token budget of the current 90-rule spec.

findings.title: Higher-order-shaped forms (sort cmp_env, parallel_for body, parallel_reduce monoid, write-protocol sinks) need effect-row polymorphism the kernel does not have — or they silently monomorphize into uselessness
findings.detail: sort(slice, cmp_env), map's find_by(env, keyview), parallel_for(slice, body_env), parallel_reduce(slice, monoid_env), and the builder's sink protocol are all functions parameterized over writer-supplied behavior. The kernel's effect rows are per-function and checked; for these forms to typecheck, either (a) the sealed op's row is polymorphic in the env's row (sort's effects = cmp's effects) — effect polymorphism, a genuinely new row-machinery feature, uncounted, and precisely the inference-flavored growth the frontend-simple ruling forbids; or (b) env methods are restricted to a fixed row (pure, or reads-only of the env's own fields), in which case: the interning composition dies (find_by's comparator must read the arena region — reads('r) with a caller-side region — through the sealed map's signature), key-extraction sorts over region data die, and parallel_for bodies cannot write their disjoint chunks unless the row somehow names a region that only exists inside the runtime's splitting. The design never states which. Also unstated: comparator lawfulness — contracts today carry exactly assoc/comm/identity, but sort correctness/termination wants a total-order law (transitivity/antisymmetry); hasher, monoid, sink, comparator are four new contract kinds, each new spec vocabulary. Evidence: write the actual signature of sort and find_by in the v0.6 row system; either it needs a row variable (new feature) or the interning PAR row and the sort PAR row are false as specified.

findings.title: The 'fail shapes are unwritable' and 'no silent perf cliff' claims are false: at least four two-spelling perf traps exist inside the catalog itself
findings.detail: (1) get-or-insert: the entry/vacancy-token idiom is the fast spelling, but find + match + insert is fully writable with the same catalog and costs two hashes+probes — a silent ~2x on exactly the counter/cache workloads the design says the entry API exists for; mid-tier models will emit find-then-insert because it matches every training-corpus prior and the entry-token discipline (affine, region-tied, consumed by insert_at, invalid across growth) is novel and fiddly. (2) INLINE knob: default 0 means the LLVM-workload 3-5x SmallVector win requires the writer to diagnose 'allocation-count-dominated' and pick N — a workload judgment the demand map itself assigns to expert reasoning; wrong-N in either direction is silent (missed 3-5x, or bloated moves/copies). (3) vec without reserve in a known-size loop: writable, taught against, silent realloc churn. (4) mutex<map> where the snapshot cell was intended: both are catalog forms, the slow one is the familiar one, and the read-scaling plateau only shows at >2-3 cores — invisible at test scale. R3 says one spelling per meaning; the catalog has one BLESSED spelling per meaning but multiple WRITABLE spellings per task, and the deltas are 2-5x, exactly the cliff class the summary claims cannot happen. Evidence: give a mid-tier model the spec alone and the task 'count word frequencies then report the top k'; measure entry-token usage rate and reserve usage rate over 20 samples. I predict <30% hit the blessed spellings first-green, against the design's implicit ~100%.

findings.title: Extensibility cliff, named and dated: the concurrent ordered map (LSM memtable) — the design courts the database audience and cannot serve its central write structure
findings.detail: The catalog ships O_DIRECT file I/O, WAL recipes, crc frames, filemap — explicitly recruiting the RocksDB/SQLite-class audience. The first structure that audience builds is a memtable: a concurrent ordered map (crossbeam-skiplist class) with concurrent inserts AND range scans. The catalog's answers all fail: mutex<omap> serializes the write path at 1 core and blocks readers during scans; sharded omap destroys cross-shard range scans (the entire reason omap exists); shared<T> snapshot-cell republishing an omap costs O(n) copy per write batch. The demand map itself names this split ('concurrent ordered maps, e.g. skiplists in LSM engines') and the design's coverage table silently absorbs it — it is a 52nd scenario with no row, not even PARTIAL. The fallback is the escalation path: petition the project for a new sealed kernel, hostile review, model checking — months of latency for a structure the user needed at design time. Same cliff class queued behind it: novel lock-free structures generally (design admits), inbound FFI plugin hosting, shared-mutable mmap. The evolution story has no compaction mechanism: each escalation permanently grows the TCB ledger (already at the 15k cap by the design's own arithmetic) AND the catalog appendix (D2's scarce resource), so the design's own success with serious systems audiences drives it through both of its stated budgets. Evidence: build a 4-writer/4-reader memtable benchmark (insert + range-scan mix) with only catalog forms vs crossbeam-skiplist; I predict >5x deficit on the mixed workload, i.e. a fail-line miss in the design's own terms.

findings.title: The dispatch attribute's 'semantic equivalence obligation across variants' is a one-clause rug over either an unchecked correctness hole or an uncounted translation-validation engine
findings.detail: SIMD core (~8 rules claimed) includes: 'the dispatch attribute (per-ISA compilation, one startup selection, semantic equivalence obligation across variants).' Who discharges the obligation? If it is unchecked (writer-asserted), then a mid-tier writer authoring the AVX2 and NEON variants of a kernel — where NEON has no movemask and the idiom must be rebuilt from shrn/umaxp — can ship variants that disagree, producing platform-dependent wrong results in fully checked code: a direct W1 violation (weak writers are told to fill in a skeleton they cannot verify) and untestable on single-ISA CI. If it IS checked, cross-ISA semantic equivalence of vectorized kernels is translation-validation-grade machinery — far beyond frontend-simple, and worth 20+ rules on its own, not a clause inside 8. The design's own mitigation ('ordinary writers CALL kernels, project authors WRITE them') concedes the form is not writer-usable, which reclassifies the SIMD core from Tier A language primitive to project-internal tooling — but then EXP-2's 'checked-source par' claim is about project-expert code, not the catalog's teachable surface. Evidence: specify the dispatch rule at kernel precision; either the equivalence obligation appears as writer-trusted (name the enforcement gap) or as a checker judgment (name the algorithm and count its rules). There is no third option.

findings.title: Every source_shape in the design is written in a language xlang is not: '?', method chains, closures-adjacent idioms — under real ANF/match-only, program mass grows 3-5x and the D2 example budget with it
findings.detail: The design's own examples use `c.varint32()?` (no ? operator exists — no operators at all), `ring.submit(...)?`, method-call chaining, and `spsc.new::<Frame>(1024)` turbofish. In actual v0.6: every fallible op is an exhaustive two-arm match in ANF with manual error propagation (and the kernel has loop+break — early-return-on-error across a 10-field protobuf decode is 10 sequential matches each threading the error to the boundary, or one deeply restructured loop). The taught examples that make the catalog learnable must be written in the real syntax, and they will be 3-5x the token count of the pseudocode — this lands on exactly the budget the design already flags as tight (catalog appendix). It is also a teachability hazard in itself: mid-tier models drift more over long mechanical spans, and the blessed cursor/decode patterns become long mechanical spans. Evidence: transcribe EXP-3's LZ4 block decoder and one protobuf-style 8-field decode into strict v0.6 ANF/match form; measure token ratio vs the design's pseudocode and count the match arms a writer must get right per field. If the ratio exceeds ~3x, the ai_writability section's claims need re-arguing against the real notation, and D2 needs a revised token budget.

findings.title: Evolution is one-way: frozen conformance suites plus taught catalog make every post-ship contract ambiguity a breaking spec edit, and the design names no versioning, deprecation, or spelling-migration mechanism
findings.detail: The TCB story freezes each kernel's op contract in a conformance suite; the catalog teaches one spelling per meaning; the design itself lists 'sealed-form op-contract creep' as a risk (entry tokens, cursor handoffs, ring completion semantics). Concretely: when the entry-token restriction (no map growth between entry() and insert_at()) proves too tight in real writer code — the design's own risk #8 — the fix is either a second entry spelling (violating R3, forking taught material, invalidating the trained decision tree) or a contract change that breaks the frozen suite and every existing program. Same pattern awaits the ring completion-ordering semantics and cursor invalidation rules. Rust survives this with editions, deprecation windows, and semver; this design has an append-only lab discipline but NO stated mechanism for retiring or revising a taught spelling — and D6 makes it worse: since forms are taught up front, a revision invalidates cached writer knowledge (including fine-tuned/prompted model behavior) wholesale. Evidence: the design should be required to state, before shipping, the procedure for changing one shipped op contract (who breaks, what migrates, what the spec diff is); if no answer fits inside D2 and R3, the catalog needs an explicit versioning rule now, counted in the spec budget.

overall: The stance is rescuable — the three-tier criterion is genuinely good, the sealed-kernel bet matches the implement-rarely/use-often structure of real systems code, and the coverage arithmetic against the demand map is mostly honest — but the design as written is a Rust-typed pseudocode catalog stapled onto a kernel that cannot type it: option<&T>, borrow-carrying enums, guards, and cursors appear in essentially every Tier B interface while the kernel forbids borrows in data, forbids reborrowing, and has no iteration or error-propagation protocol, so the 47-rule count, the "checker does nothing new" claim, and every PAR verdict are currently claims about a language that does not exist. The single change that matters most: before any experiment, design the transient-borrow/return-and-iteration protocol (how a sealed form hands a borrow, a cursor, and a failure to ANF/match-only source), re-derive all ~120 op signatures in real v0.6 notation, and republish the spec-mass count with the catalog appendix inside the D2 token budget — that one exercise will simultaneously reveal the true rule count (likely 250+, not 137), the real teachability surface, and whether the elegant "22 forms" is actually 22 forms or 120 special cases wearing a uniform.
